confidence.disclosure: Nasir al-Din Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Tusi was the most important Persian polymath of the 13th century - astronomer, mathematician, philosopher, theologian, and political adviser to two of the most powerful rulers of his age (the Ismaili masters of Alamut and the Mongol Hulagu). His life is well documented in Persian and Arabic sources of the late 13th century, especially in Rashid al-Din's Jami al-Tawarikh and the introduction to his own Zij-i Ilkhani. The major dates - his birth in Tus, his service at Alamut, the fall of Alamut to Hulagu in 1256, the founding of the Maragha observatory, and his death in Baghdad in 1274 - are firm by year, with several specific months.

sources: George Saliba, A History of Arabic Astronomy (1994)

journey.event: Born on 18 February 1201 (11 Jumada I 597 AH) in Tus in Khurasan (near modern Mashhad in northeastern Iran), into a Twelver Shia family. His father is a jurist of the Imami school. His birth horoscope is preserved.

Receives his early education in Tus from his father and his maternal uncle Nur al-Din Ali ibn Muhammad, who introduces him to Avicennan philosophy and the rationalist sciences. From an early age studies mathematics and the religious sciences in parallel.

Around 1219 travels to Nishapur to continue his studies. Studies medicine, mathematics, and philosophy with the leading masters there. The arrival of the Mongol army of Genghis Khan in Khurasan in 1220-1221 disrupts everything: Nishapur is sacked in April 1221, and many of the cities of Khurasan are destroyed.

Around 1227, in the chaos of the Mongol invasions, takes refuge with the Ismaili (Nizari, often called the Assassins) governor of Quhistan, Nasir al-Din Abd al-Rahim ibn Abi Mansur. Lives at the Ismaili courts in eastern Iran for the next thirty years, formally adhering to Ismailism (though his real allegiances are debated by scholars).

Composes his most famous work on ethics, the Akhlaq-i Nasiri (Nasirean Ethics), in Persian, dedicated to his Ismaili patron. It is a synthesis of Aristotelian, Avicennan, and Persian Islamic ethical thought and becomes the standard handbook of Persian ethics for the next several centuries.

Around the mid-1240s moves from Quhistan to the great Ismaili fortress of Alamut in the Alborz mountains north of Qazwin. Lives at Alamut as the senior intellectual figure at the court of the Ismaili imam-grand master. His personal religious sympathies remain debated; he later claims to have been held against his will.

Continues his scholarly work at Alamut. Composes recensions (tahrir) of the Greek mathematical and astronomical classics: Euclid's Elements, Apollonius's Conics, Ptolemy's Almagest, and many others. These recensions become the standard versions used in the Islamic world for centuries.

On 19 November 1256, after a short siege by the army of the Mongol prince Hulagu (grandson of Genghis Khan), the Ismaili grand master Rukn al-Din Khurshah surrenders Alamut. Al-Tusi is captured. He immediately enters Hulagu's service and is treated with great honour by the Mongol prince, who recognises his learning. The great library of Alamut, including some 400,000 manuscripts, is largely destroyed but Tusi saves what he can.

Accompanies Hulagu's army on the campaign against the Abbasid caliphate. On 10 February 1258 the Mongols enter Baghdad after a brief siege. The last Abbasid caliph al-Musta'sim is executed. Al-Tusi is later accused (probably unfairly) of having advised Hulagu to put the caliph to death. The sack of Baghdad is one of the great catastrophes of Islamic history.

On Hulagu's orders begins to build a great astronomical observatory at Maragha in Azerbaijan, which becomes the new Mongol Ilkhanid capital. Construction starts in 1259. The observatory is equipped with the largest precision instruments yet built (including a wall quadrant with a radius of 6.5 metres) and houses a library of 400,000 books gathered from across the Mongol empire.

At Maragha gathers a team of the leading mathematicians and astronomers of the age - Mu'ayyad al-Din al-Urdi from Damascus, Qutb al-Din al-Shirazi, Muhyi al-Din al-Maghribi, and others. Together they observe the heavens for twelve years to produce the Zij-i Ilkhani, a set of astronomical tables that supersede all previous ones.

Develops important new mathematical models for planetary motion - notably the 'Tusi couple', a geometric construction in which two circles produce linear motion. This and other Maragha innovations are eventually transmitted (by routes still debated) to Renaissance Europe and influence Copernicus's work in the 16th century.
journey.source: Saliba; F. Jamil Ragep, Tusi's Memoir on Astronomy

journey.event: Travels from Maragha to Baghdad in 1274 on official business for the Ilkhan Abaqa (Hulagu's son and successor). Falls ill in Baghdad and dies there on 26 June 1274 (18 Dhu al-Hijja 672 AH), aged 73. By his own request is buried in the shrine of the seventh Shia imam Musa al-Kazim at Kazimayn, just north of Baghdad, where his tomb remains to this day.
